confirmed.title: 'Knowledge' nav tab shipped to all users while llm_wiki flag defaults OFF with no opt-in UI — permanent 'still being compiled' dead end
confirmed.detail: The /wiki entry is in primary nav for everyone (CollapsibleSidebar.tsx:44, BottomNav.tsx:22), but wiki compilation only runs for users with llm_wiki=true (api/services/wikiCompilationService.js:328; cron selection api/routes/cron-wiki-compile.js:60-64), and the flag defaults to false (api/routes/feature-flags.js:29 'opt-in'). Grep confirms zero frontend references to llm_wiki — there is no opt-in surface anywhere. For a default user with no connected platforms, /wiki shows 'Your knowledge graph is still being compiled. Connect platforms and chat with your twin to build up enough data.' (WikiGraphPage.tsx:74) — a promise that can never resolve. With platforms connected, buildWikiGraphData still emits platform nodes with zero domain pages (wikiCompilationService.js:918-919), so the user sees a meaningless graph of disconnected platform dots and 'Domains 0' stats.
confirmed.evidence: feature-flags.js:29 `llm_wiki: false, // opt-in` + WikiGraphPage empty-state copy promises compilation that is flag-gated off

verdict.reasoning: Every cited line was verified against the source. (1) The 'Knowledge' nav entry exists in CollapsibleSidebar.tsx:44 (static array, no flag filtering, mapped at line 217) and BottomNav.tsx:22 (More drawer); /wiki is registered in App.tsx:302 behind ProtectedRoute only. (2) llm_wiki defaults to false (feature-flags.js:29), compileWikiPages hard-skips unless flags.llm_wiki === true (wikiCompilationService.js:328), and the cron selects only explicit llm_wiki=true rows (cron-wiki-compile.js:61-65). The manual POST /wiki/compile route calls the same gated function, so it no-ops too. (3) Repo-wide grep confirms zero frontend references to llm_wiki; Settings.tsx Personality Engine renders only 4 toggles (lines 639-678) and its state (219-224) omits llm_wiki even though the backend accepts it — so there is genuinely no opt-in surface. The client triggerWikiCompile (wikiAPI.ts:80) is dead code, never imported. The project's own audit notes admit graph data exists for the test user only because the flag was manually enabled in the DB. (4) Impact confirmed: GET /wiki/graph has no flag gate; with no platforms, zero nodes triggers WikiGraphPage.tsx:74's 'still being compiled' copy that can never resolve; with platforms, wikiCompilationService.js:918-919 pushes platform nodes unconditionally, bypassing the empty state and showing disconnected platform dots with zero domains. Severity: it does not block core flows (chat/dashboard work), but a permanent top-level nav tab leading to an unfixable dead end for 100% of real users degrades the experience and undermines trust in the product's promise. Only nitpick: on mobile the tab is in the More drawer, not the primary tab bar — immaterial to the finding.

confirmed.title: Memory fetch failures swallowed silently and misrendered as 'No memories found — Connect platforms'
confirmed.detail: fetchMemories bails with bare `return` on !res.ok (line 85) and !json.success (line 88), and the catch block is literally `// silently fail` (lines 100-101). There is no error state, toast, or retry. Because loading flips to false with memories=[], any 500/401/network failure renders the empty state 'No memories found ... Connect platforms to start building memories' (lines 323-341) — telling a user with thousands of memories that they have none and pushing them to /get-started. This violates the project's own error-handling rules and misleads on a core page. The lazy snapshots fetch (lines 122-129) has the same silent .catch(() => {}).
confirmed.evidence: if (!res.ok) return; ... if (!json.success) return; ... } catch { // silently fail }

verdict.reasoning: Verified verbatim: BrainPage.tsx line 85 `if (!res.ok) return;`, line 88 `if (!json.success) return;`, lines 100-101 `catch { // silently fail }`, and the lazy snapshots fetch (lines 122-129) ending in `.catch(() => {})`. No error state, toast, or retry exists in the component. Reachability confirmed: /brain is a primary nav destination (BottomNav 'Memories', src/components/layout/BottomNav.tsx:21), registered in App.tsx:278-286 behind only ProtectedRoute, with /memories, /memory-explorer, and /journal all redirecting to it — not dead code or feature-flagged. Upstream mitigation ruled out: authFetch (src/services/api/apiBase.ts:168-178) is a thin fetch wrapper with no 401 refresh-retry, no global interceptor, and no window.fetch patch in src/. Therefore any 500/401/network failure on GET /memories leaves memories=[] with loading=false, rendering the lines 323-341 empty state ('No memories found' / 'Connect platforms to start building memories' + Connect Platforms button to /get-started) — false information shown to users who have memories, on a core page. apiBase.ts:191-204 even documents the project's required catch-site error-handling pattern that this page violates. Not blocks-user since a refresh can recover and the rest of the page works, but it actively misleads during any backend/network failure.

confirmed.title: Reconnect CTA deep-links to /settings?reconnect=gmail|calendar but nothing handles that param
confirmed.detail: detectReconnect() builds CTAs pointing to '/settings?reconnect=gmail' and '/settings?reconnect=calendar' (lines 42-43), and the comment at line 35 claims it links to 'the Settings reconnect URL pattern that handles this'. A grep across all of src/ shows no code reads a 'reconnect' query param; src/pages/Settings.tsx never calls useSearchParams/location.search. A user whose Gmail token died clicks 'Reconnect Gmail' on a failed inbox tile and lands at the top of the generic Settings page with no reconnect flow triggered, no scroll to the platforms section, and no indication of what to do next. This is the only remediation path surfaced for failed proposals, and it is a dead link parameter.
confirmed.evidence: InboxTile.tsx:41-44 RECONNECT_TARGETS urls '/settings?reconnect=gmail' / '/settings?reconnect=calendar'; Grep for 'reconnect' over src/ shows no consumer; Settings.tsx has zero matches for useSearchParams/location.search/location.hash.

verdict.reasoning: Verified all three legs of the claim. (1) Code exists as cited: src/pages/inbox/InboxTile.tsx:41-44 builds CTAs to '/settings?reconnect=gmail' and '/settings?reconnect=calendar', and the line-35 comment falsely asserts Settings handles that pattern. (2) Reachable: /inbox is a live unflagged route (App.tsx:457); the backend really produces the triggering failureReason ('Token decryption failed - user must reconnect google_gmail') at api/services/tokenRefreshService.js:643 with real provider keys, so the CTA renders for genuine token failures. (3) No consumer: repo-wide grep shows nothing reads a 'reconnect' query param; Settings.tsx never uses useSearchParams/location.search/location.hash; TokenExpiryBanner reads only 'connected'/'provider'; even the '#connections' fallback anchor is dead because Settings section ids are 'section-platforms' etc. and scrollToSection fires only on user clicks. Impact is degrades-experience rather than blocks-user: the user lands at the top of Settings with the param silently ignored, but a working Reconnect button does exist further down the page (ConnectedPlatformsSettings.tsx:134), so manual recovery is possible — the deep-link guidance is simply broken.

confirmed.title: Goal accept, dismiss, and complete failures are completely silent — unhandled promise rejection, zero user feedback
confirmed.detail: handleAccept (line 270-273), handleDismiss (275-278) and handleComplete (265-268) have no try/catch, and the card-level wrappers use try/finally WITHOUT catch (ActiveGoalCard handleComplete lines 40-43, SuggestionCard handleAccept/handleDismiss lines 106-114). goalsAPI.json() throws on !data.success (goalsAPI.ts:42), so any 500/network failure propagates as an unhandled promise rejection. The button un-disables, the card stays put, and the user gets no toast, no error text, no retry hint — they cannot tell whether 'Accept' worked. GoalsPage imports no toast at all. Contrast with InboxPage which toasts on every failure path. This is the named core flow of this audit scope (goal accept/decline).
confirmed.evidence: GoalsPage.tsx:270-273 'async function handleAccept(id) { await acceptGoal(id); await load(); }' — no catch; SuggestionCard:106-109 'try { await onAccept(goal.id); } finally { setLoading(false); }' — no catch; no 'toast' import anywhere in GoalsPage.tsx.

verdict.reasoning: Every cited detail verified against source. GoalsPage.tsx lines 265-278: handleComplete/handleAccept/handleDismiss are bare awaits with no try/catch. Card wrappers (ActiveGoalCard 40-43, SuggestionCard 106-114) use try/finally WITHOUT catch, so they re-enable the button then let the rejection escape. goalsAPI.ts line 42 throws on !data.success (plus res.json() throws on non-JSON 500s and fetch on network failure); authFetch (apiBase.ts:168-178) adds no error handling. confirmed.title: Mixed-currency totals are raw cross-currency sums displayed under one currency symbol, and the tooltip falsely claims conversion
confirmed.detail: Backend /transactions/summary adds EUR+BRL+GBP amounts together with no FX conversion (api/routes/transactions.js:427 'totalOutflow += Math.abs(t.amount)' regardless of t.currency; returned at line 470). The frontend then renders this raw sum with the dominant currency symbol (formatCurrency(summary.total_outflow, currency) at MoneyPage.tsx:226), so EUR 100 + BRL 100 shows as 'R$ 200,00'. Worse, the 'multi-moeda' chip tooltip (MoneyPage.tsx:209) says 'Totais exibidos convertidos aproximadamente em moeda dominante' — no conversion exists anywhere in the pipeline. The headline spend number of the page is factually wrong money data for any multi-currency user (the product explicitly targets BR + EU/UK + US banks simultaneously). emotional_spend_ratio (line 455 backend) is also computed over the mixed-currency sum. Additionally the frontend derives 'dominant' by transaction count over the last 50 txs (MoneyPage.tsx:756-765) while the backend sorts currencies by outflow (transactions.js:464) — two different dominance definitions that can disagree.
confirmed.evidence: MoneyPage.tsx:209 title="...Totais exibidos convertidos aproximadamente em moeda dominante." — no conversion code exists; api/routes/transactions.js:427 'if (t.amount < 0) { totalOutflow += Math.abs(t.amount); ... }' sums across all currencies

verdict.reasoning: Every element of the claim verified. Backend (api/routes/transactions.js:424-428) sums Math.abs(t.amount) into one totalOutflow across all currencies with no FX conversion; returned as total_outflow at line 470. Frontend (src/pages/MoneyPage.tsx:226) renders it via formatCurrency(summary.total_outflow, currency) where currency=dominantCurrency (passed at line 1168), stamping one currency symbol on a mixed-currency sum. Tooltip at line 209 is verbatim 'Totais exibidos convertidos aproximadamente em moeda dominante' yet grep across api/ and src/ finds zero conversion code (no exchangeRate/fxRate/convertCurrency anywhere) — the frontend comment at lines 751-755 even admits the total is 'a raw sum across currencies'. emotional_spend_ratio (transactions.js:455) divides two mixed-currency sums, skewing the ratio by numeric magnitude (BRL ~6x EUR). Dominance mismatch confirmed: frontend uses tx count over last 50 transactions (MoneyPage.tsx:756-765), backend sorts by outflow (transactions.js:464). Route /money is registered in App.tsx:335 behind ordinary ProtectedRoute — no feature flag or admin gate — and multi-currency users are explicitly supported (TrueLayer EUR/GBP + Pluggy BRL). Impact rated degrades-experience rather than blocks-user: the headline spend number and stress ratio are factually wrong for multi-currency users and the tooltip falsely claims conversion, but a correct per-currency breakdown renders directly below (lines 267-299), and single-currency users are unaffected.

confirmed.title: Sync button is broken for TrueLayer and Plaid connections — provider never passed, request hits the Pluggy route and fails silently
confirmed.detail: handleSync calls 'await syncBankConnection(id)' without the provider argument (BankConnectionsList.tsx:80; button at line 181), so syncBankConnection (src/services/api/transactionsAPI.ts:351-363) always falls through to the default '/transactions/pluggy/sync/:id' endpoint. The backend explicitly rejects TrueLayer rows with 400 'use /api/truelayer/sync/:id' (api/routes/pluggy.js:315-319), and for Plaid rows it calls pluggy.triggerSync(row.pluggy_item_id) with a null pluggy_item_id, producing a 500. syncBankConnection returns false on !ok, handleSync ignores the return value and has no catch, so the user sees the spinner stop and nothing else — sync silently never happens for any non-Pluggy bank. Compare handleDelete (line 92-93) which correctly passes the provider.
confirmed.evidence: BankConnectionsList.tsx:80 'await syncBankConnection(id);' vs transactionsAPI.ts:354-358 provider dispatch; api/routes/pluggy.js:318 'return res.status(400).json({ success: false, error: "use /api/truelayer/sync/:id for TrueLayer connections" });'

verdict.reasoning: Every cited line checks out. BankConnectionsList.tsx:80 calls syncBankConnection(id) without the provider arg (button at line 181), while handleDelete at lines 92-93 correctly passes it. transactionsAPI.ts:351-363 dispatches on provider and defaults to /transactions/pluggy/sync/:id when undefined. The backend pluggy.js sync handler (lines 300-333) rejects TrueLayer rows with the exact quoted 400 at lines 315-319, and Plaid rows fall through the truelayer-only guard to pluggy.triggerSync(row.pluggy_item_id) with a null pluggy_item_id (Plaid rows store plaid_item_id instead), producing a PATCH to /items/null on the Pluggy API and a 500. The code is fully reachable: BankConnectionsList renders on MoneyPage (line 962) at the live /money route, the shared GET /connections endpoint is explicitly provider-agnostic and returns Pluggy + TrueLayer + Plaid rows, and both TrueLayer OAuth and Plaid Link connect flows are exposed in ConnectBankButton.tsx with their sync routes mounted (/api/truelayer/sync/:id at truelayer.js:151, /api/plaid/sync/:id at plaid.js:413). Failure is silent: syncBankConnection returns false on !ok rather than throwing, handleSync ignores the return value with no catch and no error UI — spinner stops, nothing happens. Impact is degrades-experience rather than blocks-user because webhooks and daily fallback crons (cron-plaid-sync, cron-pluggy-sync) still sync bank data in the background; only the manual sync button is broken for all non-Pluggy (EU/UK and US) connections.

verdict.reasoning: Every factual element checks out. api/routes/truelayer.js:92/95/138/144 redirect to /money?truelayer_connected=1 or /money?truelayer_error=<code> exactly as cited. ConnectBankButton.tsx:200 does window.location.assign for the EU/UK flow (full-page, no in-page callback unlike Pluggy/Plaid widgets), and the button is rendered unconditionally on the protected /money route (MoneyPage.tsx:944, App.tsx:335) with no feature flag — fully user-reachable when TrueLayer is configured. confirmed.title: Platform Disconnect button on /settings always fails: DELETE sent without auth header
confirmed.detail: handleDisconnectService calls fetch(`${API_URL}/connectors/${provider}/${user?.id}`, { method: 'DELETE' }) with NO Authorization header. The backend route router.delete('/:provider/:userId', authenticateUser, ...) (api/routes/connectors.js:1445) hard-requires the Authorization header (api/middleware/auth.js:74-81 returns 401 when missing). Result: every disconnect from Settings gets 401, response.ok is false, and the code silently calls revertOptimisticUpdate() — the platform vanishes from the list for a moment, then reappears, with zero error message. The user cannot disconnect any platform from Settings. The parallel implementation in src/pages/components/onboarding/usePlatformConnect.ts:423-431 correctly sends the Bearer token, proving the omission. The catch/else paths (lines 290-294) swallow the failure with no toast.
confirmed.evidence: Settings.tsx:287 `const response = await fetch(`${API_URL}/connectors/${provider}/${user?.id}`, { method: 'DELETE' });` vs connectors.js:1445 `router.delete('/:provider/:userId', authenticateUser, ...)` and auth.js:78-81 `log.warn('Missing authorization header'...) ... 'Missing or invalid authorization header'`

verdict.reasoning: Verified every link in the chain. (1) Settings.tsx:287 sends DELETE to ${API_URL}/connectors/${provider}/${user?.id} with no headers object at all, while every other fetch in the same file uses getAuthHeaders() — a clear one-off omission. (2) No global fetch interceptor exists in src/ to inject the Authorization header. (3) server.js:584 mounts connectors.js at /api/connectors, and connectors.js:1445 (router.delete('/:provider/:userId', authenticateUser, ...)) is the only matching route; auth.js:77-83 returns 401 immediately when the Authorization header is absent, with no cookie fallback. (4) The flow is live user-facing code: /settings is routed in App.tsx:386-394 behind ProtectedRoute, and ConnectedPlatformsSettings.tsx:112-118 wires the Disconnect button to handleDisconnectService with no feature flag. (5) Impact confirmed: response.ok is false, so lines 290-294 silently call revertOptimisticUpdate() — platform vanishes optimistically then reappears, with no toast or error. The parallel implementation in usePlatformConnect.ts:423-431 correctly sends Bearer ${getAccessToken()}, proving the backend works when the header is supplied. Every disconnect attempt from Settings fails with 401, 100% reproducible. Rated blocks-user because the Settings disconnect flow is completely non-functional with zero feedback; the only workaround (disconnecting via the onboarding connect surface) is non-obvious and undiscoverable from the silent failure.

confirmed.title: Twin Intelligence section crashes entire Settings page when readiness query errors; infinite spinner on non-OK response
confirmed.detail: Two failure modes: (1) fetchReadiness (lines 37-42) returns null when /finetuning/readiness responds non-OK (401/500), so readiness=null and readinessError=false forever — the Model Status (line 188) and Training Data (line 240) rows show endless spinners with no error state and no retry. (2) If the fetch throws (network error, after retry:1), isError=true and data=undefined; the spinner guard `(loadingReadiness || (!readiness && !readinessError))` evaluates false, and the IIFE at line 248 executes `const count = readiness.conversations || 0;` on undefined — TypeError that unmounts the whole Settings page into the route ErrorBoundary. One flaky request to a secondary stats endpoint takes down all of Settings.
confirmed.evidence: Line 37-42: `if (!res.ok) return null;` then line 240-248: `{(loadingReadiness || (!readiness && !readinessError)) ? <Loader2/> : (() => { const count = readiness.conversations || 0;` — readiness can be undefined when isError is true

verdict.reasoning: Verified both failure modes against the actual code. (1) src/pages/components/settings/TwinIntelligence.tsx:37-42 returns null on non-OK because authFetch (src/services/api/apiBase.ts:168) never throws on HTTP errors; React Query v5.83 treats null as successful data, so readiness=null, readinessError=false, loadingReadiness=false — the guard `(loadingReadiness || (!readiness && !readinessError))` at lines 188 and 240 stays true forever, leaving permanent spinners on the Model Status and Training Data rows with no error state or retry. The backend route (api/routes/finetuning.js:347) can return 401 (authenticateUser, expired token — authFetch has no 401 refresh) or 500. (2) On fetch rejection (network error), after retry:1 the query settles with isError=true, data=undefined; the guard evaluates false ((!undefined && !true) = false), so the IIFE at line 248 executes `readiness.conversations` on undefined — a render-phase TypeError. Line 188's branch is safe via optional chaining, but line 248 has none. The throw is caught by the per-route ErrorBoundary wrapping <Settings /> (App.tsx:389), replacing the entire Settings page with the error fallback. Reachability confirmed: TwinIntelligence renders unconditionally at Settings.tsx:562 on the protected /settings route — no feature flag or admin gate; bare `new QueryClient()` (src/lib/queryClient.ts) means no defaults alter this. Severity: degrades-experience rather than blocks-user — the crash requires a network-level fetch failure twice in a row and is recoverable via the boundary's retry/reload; the infinite-spinner mode (server 401/500) leaves the rest of Settings functional.

confirmed.title: Privacy controls show success toast before the save happens; all privacy mutations fail silently
confirmed.detail: handleGlobalCommit (line 246-254) and handlePresetApply (line 256-263) fire toast.success('Global privacy set to X%') synchronously when calling updateSettings — before the PUT resolves. handleActivateTwin (lines 283-287) likewise toasts 'Twin activated' before the POST settles. None of the mutations in src/hooks/usePrivacySettings.ts (updateSettings:53-75, updateClusterPrivacy:323-345, toggleCluster:348-370, activateTwin:223-244, deactivateAllTwins:247-268) define an onError handler, so a failed save is never surfaced — the user is explicitly told their privacy level changed when it may not have. For a product whose stated philosophy is 'the privacy spectrum dashboard IS the trust builder', a false 'privacy saved' confirmation is a trust-breaking bug. Per-cluster slider changes and cluster toggles give no feedback at all on failure.
confirmed.evidence: PrivacySpectrumDashboard.tsx:249-251 `updateSettings({ globalPrivacy: level } ...); toast.success(`Global privacy set to ${level}%`);` + usePrivacySettings.ts mutations contain onSuccess only, no onError anywhere in the 488-line file

verdict.reasoning: Verified every element of the claim against the actual code. (1) PrivacySpectrumDashboard.tsx:246-263 fires toast.success synchronously immediately after calling fire-and-forget .mutate for global privacy and presets; handleActivateTwin (279-290) does the same for twin activate/deactivate; cluster slider/toggle handlers give no feedback at all. (2) usePrivacySettings.ts (read in full, 488 lines) contains onSuccess handlers only — zero onError on any of the five mutations, and the hooks export only .mutate (not mutateAsync), so callers cannot catch failures. (3) No safety net exists: src/lib/queryClient.ts is a bare `new QueryClient()` (no MutationCache onError), mutations default throwOnError:false so the route ErrorBoundary never fires, and ErrorNotification reads a separate ErrorContext these hooks never touch. (4) Fully user-reachable: routed at /privacy-spectrum behind ordinary ProtectedRoute, linked from Settings.tsx:723, redirected from /settings/privacy, Sonner Toaster mounted in App.tsx:142 so the premature toasts actually render. On a failed save the user sees a success toast, local state (setGlobalLevel) keeps showing the new value, and no error ever surfaces — a false 'privacy saved' confirmation in the product's core trust surface. Rated degrades-experience rather than blocks-user because the happy path works correctly; the harm occurs only when a save request fails, but when it does, the user is actively misled about their privacy state with no way to notice.

confirmed.title: Public pricing contradicts in-app pricing and actual backend limits
confirmed.detail: The public /discover pricing section advertises Free = 'Up to 3 platforms' + '1,000 monthly memories', Plus = 'All platforms connected' + 'Unlimited memories'. The in-app /pricing page (src/pages/PricingPage.tsx:15-37) says Free = '2 platform connections' + '100 chat messages / month', Plus = '5 platform connections' + '1,500 chat messages / month'. The backend source of truth (api/services/subscriptionService.js:13-29 PLAN_LIMITS) is 2 platforms/100 msgs free and 5 platforms/1500 msgs plus — so the public page overstates Free by 50% and sells Plus features ('all platforms', 'unlimited memories') that do not exist at that tier. Two pricing surfaces for the same product directly disagree.
confirmed.evidence: DiscoverPricing.tsx:16 features: ['Up to 3 platforms', '1,000 monthly memories', ...]; :25 ['All platforms connected', 'Unlimited memories', ...] vs subscriptionService.js PLAN_LIMITS free:{chatMessages:100, platformConnections:2}, pro:{chatMessages:1500, platformConnections:5}

verdict.reasoning: Every cited fact checks out. DiscoverPricing.tsx:16/25 advertises Free='Up to 3 platforms'+'1,000 monthly memories' and Plus='All platforms connected'+'Unlimited memories' on the public, unauthenticated /discover route (App.tsx:182, rendered unconditionally at DiscoverLanding.tsx:271). The in-app /pricing page (PricingPage.tsx:8-61, routed at App.tsx:407) and the enforced backend source of truth (subscriptionService.js PLAN_LIMITS: free=2 platforms/100 msgs/7-day memory; pro->'Plus'=5 platforms/1500 msgs/90-day memory; PLAN_DISPLAY_NAMES confirms pro displays as Plus) both contradict it. Limits are live, not aspirational: twinChatPreFlightChecks.js:142 enforces the chat quota via checkMonthlyQuota/getMonthlyUsage. Identical price points ($0/$20/$100) across both pages prove it's the same product with divergent feature claims. The public page overstates Free platforms (3 vs 2), invents a nonexistent '1,000 monthly memories' allowance, and sells Pro-tier ($100 max) features — all platforms, unlimited memory — under the $20 Plus plan, whose real limits are 5 of 10 platforms and a 90-day memory window. Impact: paying customers receive materially less than the public page promises (misrepresentation/refund/trust risk), and the two pricing surfaces visibly disagree for anyone who sees both. It does not block product usage, so degrades-experience rather than blocks-user.

confirmed.title: Annual billing advertised publicly but no annual checkout exists anywhere
confirmed.detail: The Monthly/Annually toggle promises Plus at '$15/mo, Billed annually ($180/yr)' and Pro at '$75/mo ($900/yr)'. The billing backend (api/routes/billing.js:255-276) maps exactly two plans to two monthly Stripe price IDs via priceIdForDbPlan; there is no annual plan key, no annual price env var, and POST /api/billing/checkout only accepts plan 'plus'|'pro'. The CTA buttons just navigate('/auth') (line 122), so a user lured by annual pricing can never actually buy it — the in-app /pricing page doesn't even show an annual option.
confirmed.evidence: DiscoverPricing.tsx:23 price: { monthly: '$20/mo', annual: '$15/mo' }, :24 sub annual 'Billed annually ($180/yr)'; billing.js PLAN_DISPLAY_TO_DB = { plus: 'pro', pro: 'max' } with only STRIPE_PRICE_PRO / STRIPE_PRICE_MAX monthly price IDs

verdict.reasoning: Every element of the claim verified. DiscoverPricing.tsx:23-24/32-33 advertises Plus at $15/mo "Billed annually ($180/yr)" and Pro at $75/mo "($900/yr)" behind a functional Monthly/Annually toggle on the public /discover route (App.tsx:182, AuthContext public allowlist). CTAs only navigate('/auth'). The billing backend (billing.js:255-276) has exactly two plans mapped to one Stripe price ID each — code comments explicitly identify them as the $20 and $100 monthly tiers — and POST /api/billing/checkout accepts only { plan: 'plus'|'pro' } with no billing-interval parameter. Grep for annual/interval/year across api/ finds no annual billing support. Both in-app purchase surfaces (PricingPage.tsx, PaywallModal.tsx) are monthly-only, and PaywallModal.tsx:136 even falsely states "Annual plans available — 2 months free." A user lured by annual pricing genuinely cannot purchase it anywhere. Classified degrades-experience (not blocks-user) because monthly checkout works; the harm is false advertising and an unpurchasable promised discount, a trust/compliance issue rather than a functional blocker.

confirmed.title: Upgrade button fails silently on every error path (try/finally with no catch, no user feedback)
confirmed.detail: handleUpgrade has try{...}finally{...} with no catch and no error UI. If /api/billing/checkout returns 503 ('Billing not configured' when STRIPE_SECRET_KEY or price IDs are missing), 400, 429, or 500, data.url is undefined and the function exits silently — the spinner resets and nothing happens. If fetch itself rejects, the async onClick produces an unhandled promise rejection. The boolean return of safeRedirect(data.url) is also ignored, so a blocked redirect is silent too. On the monetization page, a user clicking 'Upgrade to Pro' can get zero feedback of any kind.
confirmed.evidence: lines 72-82: try { const res = await fetch(...); const data = await res.json(); if (data.url) safeRedirect(data.url); } finally { setLoading(null); } — no catch, no else, no toast

verdict.reasoning: Verified exactly as claimed. src/pages/PricingPage.tsx:68-83 contains handleUpgrade with try/finally, no catch, no else, no error state, no toast — on error responses (data.url undefined) the spinner resets and nothing happens; on fetch rejection the un-awaited async onClick (line 188) yields an unhandled promise rejection; safeRedirect's boolean return (src/lib/safeRedirect.ts:20) is ignored at line 79. The page is live: routed at /pricing (src/App.tsx:407), linked from Settings.tsx (274, 597) and LimitReachedBanner.tsx (69), which is the chat-limit upsell funnel. Backend api/routes/billing.js /checkout (281-348) confirms the cited error paths: 400 'Invalid plan' (286), 503 'Billing not configured' when stripe is null (289) or price env vars missing (295), 500 on Stripe/DB errors (347), 429 via billingMutateLimiter — all return JSON without 'url'. No fetch wrapper, interceptor, or error boundary mitigates this (raw fetch is used). Rated degrades-experience rather than blocks-user because the happy path works when Stripe is configured; only on error/misconfiguration does the user get zero feedback — but on the monetization page that silent failure is a real, user-facing defect.

confirmed.title: Plan state hardcoded — page always shows Free as 'Current plan' regardless of actual subscription
confirmed.detail: The Free card is hardcoded cta:'Current plan', ctaDisabled:true and the paid CTAs are always active. The page never fetches the user's subscription even though GET /api/billing/subscription exists (api/routes/billing.js:228) and a ready-made hook consumes it (src/hooks/useSubscription.ts:14, also used by Settings.tsx:247). A Plus or Pro subscriber visiting /pricing sees 'Current plan' under Free, an active 'Upgrade to Plus' button for the plan they already pay for, and can open a duplicate Stripe checkout session.
confirmed.evidence: PLANS[0]: cta: 'Current plan', ctaDisabled: true (lines 21-22); no useSubscription/fetch of billing state anywhere in the file; useSubscription.ts:14 fetches `${API_URL}/billing/subscription`

verdict.reasoning: All cited code verified. PricingPage.tsx:21-22 hardcodes cta:'Current plan'/ctaDisabled:true on Free and the full file contains no subscription fetch — plan state is purely static. The page is live: App.tsx:407 routes /pricing behind ProtectedRoute only (no flag/admin gate), linked from LimitReachedBanner.tsx:69 and Settings.tsx:274. GET /api/billing/subscription exists at billing.js:228 and useSubscription.ts:14 fetches it (minor audit imprecision: Settings.tsx:247 fetches the endpoint inline rather than via the hook, which is actually unused — substance unaffected). Impact confirmed: the /checkout handler (billing.js:281-349) never checks existing plan/status, so a Plus/Pro subscriber sees 'Current plan' under Free, gets an active upgrade button for their own plan, and can open a real Stripe checkout; completing it would create a second Stripe subscription whose webhook upsert overwrites stripe_subscription_id, orphaning the old still-billing one (double-billing risk). Not blocking (paying users can still use the app and Settings shows correct plan), but misleading billing UI with a financial-harm path clearly degrades the experience.

mediumLow.title: 'New Chat' does not clear persisted history — old thread resurrects on refresh into a different conversation
mediumLow.detail: handleNewChat (lines 265-269) sets messages=[] and conversationId=null but never removes CHAT_HISTORY_KEY from localStorage, and the persistence effect (lines 176-178) only writes when `messages.length > 0`, so the old thread's last 20 messages stay stored. After a refresh, loadChatHistory restores the OLD messages while conversationId is gone (sessionStorage cleared by the conversationId effect), so the next send creates a brand-new server conversation visually fused onto the stale thread. handleClearChat (544-549) does this correctly — handleNewChat (also passed to ConversationList's New Chat button) is missing the same `localStorage.removeItem(CHAT_HISTORY_KEY)`. Related: history lives in localStorage but conversationId in sessionStorage, so any new tab reproduces the same mismatch.

mediumLow.area: Goals progress bar vs real data
mediumLow.title: ProgressBar ignores target_operator — inverted/meaningless for '<=' goals (e.g. meeting_count cap)
mediumLow.detail: ProgressBar computes pct = min(100, current/target) (lines 25-35) and is fed last_measured_value vs target_value (line 64-66) with no regard for goal.target_operator. The backend explicitly generates '<=' goals — goalTrackingService.js:578 documents 'meeting_count (from Calendar) - number of meetings per day, target operator <=' and the suggestion JSON schema includes target_operator. For a 'max 4 meetings/day' goal, a day with 8 meetings renders a 100% full bar (looks like success while the user is blowing the cap), and a good day with 2 meetings renders a half-empty bar. The backend evaluateTarget() (goalTrackingService.js:804) handles the operator correctly for streaks, so the bar visibly contradicts the streak data next to it.
mediumLow.evidence: GoalsPage.tsx:26 'const pct = Math.min(100, Math.round((current / Math.max(target, 1)) * 100));' — no operator branch; goalTrackingService.js:578 'meeting_count ... target operator <='; goalsAPI.ts:11 target_operator?: '>=' | '<=' | ... exists on the Goal type but is never read in GoalsPage.

mediumLow.area: Inbox department status panel / broken default state
mediumLow.title: Documented 'default expanded when pending items exist' behavior can never trigger on first load
mediumLow.detail: The header comment (lines 14-15) promises 'default expanded when the user has pending items waiting (they're in /inbox to triage)'. But open is initialized with useState(pendingCount > 0) (line 69), and the panel is mounted by InboxPage (InboxPage.tsx:271) before the inbox query resolves, when pendingCount — computed from data?.items ?? [] — is always 0. useState initializers run once, so when the stream loads with pending items the prop change is ignored and the panel stays collapsed. On any fresh page load the documented auto-expand never happens; it only works when React Query already has cached items at mount.

mediumLow.detail: The Undo toast's async onClick (lines 175-185) awaits departmentsAPI.undoProposal(id). undoProposal handles non-OK HTTP responses (returns {success:false}), but a thrown fetch/network error from authFetch propagates out of the un-try/catch'd onClick — the user taps Undo on a flaky connection and nothing happens: no 'Undone', no error toast, plus an unhandled promise rejection in console.

mediumLow.title: Stress-spend timeline on /money/insights formats amounts with the SUBSCRIPTIONS currency (default USD), not the transactions' currency
mediumLow.detail: StressSpendTimeline receives currency={subsCurrency} (line 276), where subsCurrency comes from the recurring-subscriptions response and defaults to 'USD' (line 79, and the backend returns 'USD' whenever no subscriptions are detected — transactions.js:1151). A BRL user with no detected subscriptions sees their daily spend axis and tooltips rendered as USD. On /money the same chart correctly receives the tx-derived dominantCurrency (MoneyPage.tsx:978). The timeline data itself (getTimelineAnalysis) carries no currency, so picking the subscriptions' currency is an arbitrary and frequently wrong source.

mediumLow.title: Feedback toggle is optimistic with no failure handling: failed saves keep the wrong UI state, network errors are unhandled rejections
mediumLow.detail: toggle() sets the value optimistically (line 331) then awaits setTransactionFeedback(...).finally(...) with no catch (line 334). setTransactionFeedback returns false on a non-OK response (transactionsAPI.ts:606-615) and that boolean is ignored — the chip stays toggled even though nothing was saved. If authFetch throws (network down), the rejection propagates out of the async toggle invoked as 'void toggle(true)' (lines 355/366), producing an unhandled promise rejection with zero user feedback and no revert.

mediumLow.title: Sync and disconnect swallow all failures — boolean results ignored, no catch, no user feedback on error
mediumLow.detail: handleSync (lines 77-86) and handleDelete (lines 88-99) use try/finally with no catch: a thrown network error becomes an unhandled rejection. Both syncBankConnection and deleteBankConnection return false on HTTP failure (transactionsAPI.ts:345-348, 359-362) and both results are discarded, so a failed disconnect/sync just stops the spinner and re-renders the unchanged list — the user has no way to know the action failed. load() at line 68-71 likewise has no error handling (listBankConnections returns [] on !ok but throws on network failure).
mediumLow.evidence: BankConnectionsList.tsx:80-82 'await syncBankConnection(id); await load(); onChanged?.();' — no catch, result unused

mediumLow.title: HTTP errors are converted into 'no data' across the money API layer — server failures render as innocent empty states

On MoneyPage this means a 500 from nudge-stats shows the cheerful 'Ainda sem avisos' empty state (MoneyPage.tsx:531-557), a failed patterns call silently removes the 'Seus padrões' card, and a failed timeline just hides the moat chart — the user can never distinguish 'you have no data' from 'the server is broken'. mediumLow.title: DataRevealItem imported but never rendered — discovered data points are never itemized in the reveal
mediumLow.detail: DiscoverLanding painstakingly builds a dataPoints array (name, company, location, bio, platform hits) but DiscoverHero only ever uses its length; the DataRevealItem component import is dead and the 'instant wow' reveal shows a generic count sentence instead of the found items, undercutting the page's core promise.
mediumLow.evidence: line 3: import DataRevealItem from '../../onboarding/components/DataRevealItem'; — zero usages in the component body; dataPoints only referenced as dataPoints.length (lines 82, 147, 184-185)

mediumLow.title: Portfolio API returns title and location that the page never renders
mediumLow.detail: The public endpoint aggregates enriched_profiles.discovered_title and discovered_location (api/routes/portfolio-public.js:143-144) and the PortfolioData interface declares them, but no portfolio component receives or displays them — PortfolioHero only takes firstName/avatar/archetype/platforms. Either render them or stop exposing extra personal data on an unauthenticated endpoint.
mediumLow.evidence: PortfolioPage.tsx:24-25 title/location in interface; PortfolioHero props (PortfolioHero.tsx:17-24) have no title/location; no other component consumes portfolio.title or portfolio.location

mediumLow.title: Raw email addresses sent as PostHog event properties on public funnels
mediumLow.detail: trackFunnel('beta_waitlist_joined', { email }) on the waitlist page, and trackFunnel('discovery_confirmed'/'discovery_not_me', { email }) on DiscoverLanding (lines 129, 134), push pre-signup visitor email addresses into product analytics. For a product whose stated differentiator is 'Privacy as Feature', shipping PII of people who have not even created accounts into PostHog is off-brand and a GDPR-surface increase.
mediumLow.evidence: WaitlistPage.tsx:35 trackFunnel('beta_waitlist_joined', { email: email.trim() }); DiscoverLanding.tsx:129 trackFunnel('discovery_confirmed', { email: email.trim() })
